experiment_name: V9 formal trial-by-trial honesty x return-policy control

return_policies.label: Fair/high return
return_policies.description: A partner policy with positive expected value for investment.

return_policies.label: Unfair/low return
return_policies.description: A partner policy with negative expected value for investment.

presentation_modes.label: Sequential trial-by-trial
presentation_modes.description: One continuous conversation; the model decides, receives feedback, and continues.

presentation_modes.label: Evidence-only independent trial
presentation_modes.description: Each trial is an independent API call with compressed objective summaries only.

blocks.label: Main: partner factual honesty
blocks.purpose: Core 2 x 2 x presentation test: factual honesty is crossed with actual return policy.

blocks.label: Control: no statement
blocks.purpose: Baseline investment when no verifiable statement is available.

blocks.label: Control: irrelevant truth
blocks.purpose: Tests whether truthfulness about task-irrelevant facts is overgeneralized to investment.

blocks.label: Control: cheap talk only
blocks.purpose: Tests whether supportive promises without verifiable truth feedback move investment.

primary_contrasts: Within each return policy and presentation mode, compare high vs low factual honesty in main_partner_honesty.
Compare main_partner_honesty with no_statement_control to estimate baseline shift.
Compare main_partner_honesty with irrelevant_truth_control to test whether honesty is treated as a social signal or overgeneralized factual correctness.
Compare standard vs explicit_orthogonality inside main_partner_honesty.
Compare cheap_talk_only_control with no_statement_control to estimate pure language cue effects.
analysis_plan.trial_level_model: investment_t ~ cumulative_truth_rate_before + cumulative_return_rate_before + previous_payoff + trial + statement_mode + return_policy + presentation_mode + orthogonality_instruction + model
analysis_plan.future_multimodel_extension: Add model as a fixed effect or random intercept once multiple model families are run.
